mnemonics.title: Date Format Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: Y M D H M S: Year, Month, Day, Hour, Minute, Second - Remember as %Y%m%d%H%M%S gives chronological order from largest to smallest unit.

mnemonics.title: Format Specifier Memory Trick
mnemonics.content: Remember: Y(ear), m(onth), d(ay) - lowercase vs UPPERCASE: lowercase gives numbers, UPPERCASE gives names. %m = month number, %M = minute. %b = abbreviated month, %B = full month.

acronyms.usage: Use date -u to display UTC time independent of timezone settings. UTC is the primary time standard.
International date/time format standard: YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ. Use date +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ for ISO 8601 compliance with UTC.
Environment variable that affects date output: TZ=America/New_York date displays Eastern time. Set to empty string for UTC.

visualTips.visualization: Picture a timeline starting at 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC. Every second adds 1 to the count. Current epoch ~1.7 billion seconds represents about 54 years (2024).
Think of date format as a template: date +"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S" → Think of it as: YEAR-MONTH-DAY SPACE HOUR:MINUTE:SECOND, all arranged in a calendar/clock visual.

analogies.analogy: The format string in date is like a pizza order: +FORMAT tells the chef (date) exactly how to serve the toppings (year, month, day, etc.) in what order.
Changing system date with 'date -s' is like changing the master clock of a school - only the principal (root) has the key to do it, not students (regular users).
